original_abstract:
This article examines the evolution of literary tourism from its roots in the European Grand Tour to its manifestation in nineteenth-century colonial Africa. By analyzing the intersection of travel habits, Romanticism, and the Industrial Revolution, the study illustrates how the cultural process of "establishing" Africa as a tourism destination was heavily mediated by literary tropes. During this era, European perceptions of the continent were filtered through aesthetic lenses such as the sublime and the Gothic, giving rise to persistent cultural constructs including the "dark continent," the "noble savage," and the "hunter’s paradise."

The paper specifically explores the literature-tourism relationship by focusing on the works of H. Rider Haggard and their connection to Great Zimbabwe. Although Haggard did not explicitly base his settings on the ruins, popular imagination and colonial promotional efforts inextricably linked the site to his imperial adventure fiction, such as *King Solomon’s Mines* and *She*. Through this case study, the article demonstrates how historical and literary developments converged to transform physical Southern African landscapes into "lieux d’imagination"—symbolic, alluring destinations that invited readers to experience a fictionalized reality. Ultimately, the research highlights how nineteenth-century adventure narratives shaped the foundational tourist gaze toward Southern Africa, creating a legacy of literary pilgrimage that persists in the region’s heritage landscape.
humanized_abstract:
This article traces the development of literary tourism from the beginnings of the Grand Tour in Europe to its manifestation in colonial Africa in the nineteenth century. Examining the interplay of habits of travel, Romanticism, and the Industrial Revolution that constituted the culture that enabled the "making visible" of Africa as a real tourist destination, this article demonstrates how representations constituted the literary topography that tourists brought with them to explore the continent. Influenced by prevailing Romantic-era literary ideas such as the sublimity and the grotesque, these representations embedded themselves deeply within culture creating enduring stereotypes of Africa as the "dark continent", the "noble savage" and a "hunter’s paradise".

Tourism and literature have long interplayed in numerous ways creating various destinations that are open to multiple readings and understandings. This paper thus focuses on literature that related to tourism in the colonial period and examining how they constructed the Southern African destinations for the new class of tourists that were emerging at the time. Special attention is given to the literature of H. Rider Haggard and how it represented Great Zimbabwe, albeit that the author did not set any of his fiction in Great Zimbabwe, the latter appears so prominently in *King Solomon’s Mines* and *She*, that the site of the ancient city cannot but evoke those aspects of the two novels that describe the discoveries of the mine of Solomon and the Land of Woot, homelands of the legend of *She*. The paper demonstrates by means of this literary example how the colonial tourism was made possible through interplay of the literary and historical forces. Moreover it illustrates how the Southern African landscapes were made into what French historian Pierre Nora terms *lieux d’imagination* — attractions which capture people’s minds and which are thus in effect places for dreams into which tourists can escape for a while. This paper shows that it was through Haggard’s colonial adventure fiction that the 19th-century tourists got their first taste of Southern Africa as an attraction in its own right. That is, 19th-century literature of adventure made the Southern Africa a ready-made destination for the tourism that was increasingly emerging and thus creating the literary tourist route in the Southern African region that has continued in the tradition up to this present day.

This article examines the evolution of literary tourism from its roots in the European Grand Tour to its manifestation in nineteenth-century colonial Africa.
